questions.question: What is the N+1 select problem?
options: Loading N parent rows triggers N additional queries for children
questions.explanation: E.g., fetch 100 Orders (1 query) then lazy-load each Order's items (100 queries). Fix with join fetch, @EntityGraph, or batch size.

questions.question: What does FetchType.LAZY mean for @OneToMany?
options: Collection loads when accessed and session is open
questions.explanation: LAZY proxies the collection until accessed. Access outside an open session causes LazyInitializationException unless initialized earlier.

questions.question: What does FetchType.EAGER imply?
options: Associated data loaded with parent; can cause large joins or cartesian products
questions.explanation: EAGER fetches association with parent load. Convenient but can over-fetch and hurt performance—often prefer LAZY + explicit fetch plans.

questions.question: Which JPQL keyword reduces N+1 when loading authors and books?
questions.explanation: JOIN FETCH loads association in the same query: SELECT a FROM Author a JOIN FETCH a.books

questions.question: What is Hibernate batch fetching (e.g., @BatchSize)?
options: Loading multiple lazy collections or entities in fewer SQL round-trips
questions.explanation: @BatchSize on class or collection lets Hibernate load multiple uninitialized proxies/collections in one IN-clause query.

questions.question: What does CascadeType.REMOVE do?
options: Deleting parent may delete associated children per mapping
questions.explanation: REMOVE cascades delete to associated entities—dangerous on @ManyToOne if misapplied. OrphanRemoval handles unlinked children in @OneToMany.

questions.question: What is orphanRemoval = true on @OneToMany?
options: Children without parent reference are deleted when relationship changes
questions.explanation: orphanRemoval deletes child rows removed from the parent's collection when the unit of work flushes—distinct from cascade REMOVE semantics.

questions.question: Which cascade type propagates merge from parent to children?
questions.explanation: MERGE reattaches detached graphs: merging parent can merge children if cascade includes MERGE.

questions.question: What is the first-level (L1) cache in Hibernate?
options: Session-scoped persistence context; identity map for entities in that Session
questions.explanation: Each Session/EntityManager has an L1 cache: repeated get by id within same transaction returns same instance without extra SELECT.

questions.question: What does second-level (L2) cache require?
options: Nothing; enabled by default for all entities
Provider (e.g., Ehcache), entity/cache region configuration, often read-mostly data
questions.explanation: L2 is SessionFactory-scoped shared cache across sessions. Configure cache provider, @Cacheable entities, and concurrency strategy.

questions.question: When is query cache useful?
options: When identical queries repeat with same parameters and underlying data changes infrequently
questions.explanation: Query cache stores result ids; still needs L2 for entities. Invalidate carefully when data changes or use short TTL.

questions.question: What does @Cache(usage = CacheConcurrencyStrategy.READ_ONLY) suggest?
options: Data is immutable after insert; safe optimized read cache
questions.explanation: READ_ONLY suits reference data never updated. READ_WRITE/NONSTRICT_READ_WRITE handle updates with versioning considerations.

questions.question: What is Dependency Injection in Spring?
options: Developers always use new in constructors manually
Spring container provides dependencies to objects instead of them creating collaborators
questions.explanation: IoC container wires beans via constructor, setter, or field injection based on configuration (XML, annotations, Java config).

questions.question: Which injection style is generally recommended in modern Spring?
questions.explanation: Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit, eases unit testing, and allows immutable fields. Field injection is harder to test without Spring.

questions.question: What does @Component stereotype indicate?
options: A Spring-managed bean detected by component scanning
questions.explanation: @Component is generic stereotype. Specializations: @Service, @Repository, @Controller.

questions.question: Difference between @Component and @Bean?
options: @Component on class; @Bean on method in @Configuration defining factory-created instance
questions.explanation: @Bean methods programmatically register objects (third-party classes). @Component marks your class for classpath scanning.

questions.question: What does @Autowired do?
options: Injects matching bean by type (and qualifier/name if needed)
questions.explanation: @Autowired resolves dependencies from the ApplicationContext. Use @Qualifier or @Primary when multiple beans of same type exist.

questions.question: When are two beans of the same interface ambiguous?
options: When multiple implementations exist without @Primary or @Qualifier
questions.explanation: Spring throws NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException unless @Primary marks default or @Qualifier disambiguates injection point.

questions.question: What does @Scope("prototype") mean?
options: New instance created each time bean is requested/injected (prototype)
questions.explanation: Default singleton = one shared instance. Prototype creates a new object per getBean/injection—watch memory with long-lived singletons holding prototypes.

questions.question: What happens during Spring bean initialization order broadly?
options: Instantiate → populate properties → BeanPostProcessor before init → @PostConstruct/custom init → ready
questions.explanation: Container creates bean, injects dependencies, applies BeanPostProcessors, calls initialization callbacks (@PostConstruct, InitializingBean), then bean is usable.

questions.question: Which annotation marks a method to run after dependency injection?
questions.explanation: @PostConstruct (javax/jakarta.annotation) runs after injection. @PreDestroy runs on shutdown for cleanup.

questions.question: What is a BeanPostProcessor?
options: Callback hook to customize beans before/after initialization
questions.explanation: BeanPostProcessors can wrap or modify beans (e.g., @Autowired processing, AOP proxies) in postProcessBefore/AfterInitialization.

questions.question: What does @Configuration provide?
options: Declares class as source of @Bean definitions and CGLIB-enhanced singleton semantics
questions.explanation: @Configuration classes define @Bean methods. Full @Configuration CGLIB-proxies @Bean methods so singletons call each other correctly.

questions.question: What does @ComponentScan do?
options: Registers @Component and stereotype classes in specified base packages
questions.explanation: @ComponentScan on @SpringBootApplication or @Configuration tells Spring where to find annotated beans.

questions.question: What is ApplicationContext?
options: Spring IoC container providing bean factory, events, internationalization, and more
questions.explanation: ApplicationContext extends BeanFactory with enterprise features: event publication, resource loading, and environment abstraction.

questions.question: Which constructor will Spring use for injection?
options: The single constructor with parameters (implicit autowire in Spring 4.3+)
questions.explanation: If a class has one constructor, Spring autowires it without @Autowired. Multiple constructors need @Autowired on chosen one.

questions.question: What is the default scope for Spring beans?
questions.explanation: Unless @Scope specifies otherwise, beans are singletons within the container (one instance per Spring context, not JVM-global static).

questions.question: What causes LazyInitializationException?
options: Accessing uninitialized lazy association outside persistence context/session
questions.explanation: Close session then call order.getItems().size() → exception. Fix: fetch join, @Transactional service method, or DTO projection.

questions.question: What does @EntityGraph accomplish?
options: Declares fetch plan (attributes to load) for repository query
questions.explanation: @EntityGraph on Spring Data method specifies which associations to fetch eagerly for that query without changing global EAGER mapping.

questions.question: What does @PreDestroy indicate?
options: Cleanup hook before bean removal from container
Run before each HTTP request always
questions.explanation: @PreDestroy methods release resources (close connections) when the context shuts down or bean is discarded.

questions.question: What is @Qualifier used for?
options: Select specific bean when multiple candidates share a type
questions.explanation: @Qualifier("emailNotifier") pairs with @Autowired to inject the named bean implementation.

questions.question: Why use @Repository stereotype?
options: Indicates persistence layer; enables exception translation to DataAccessException
questions.explanation: @Repository is semantic @Component plus persistence exception translation via PersistenceExceptionTranslationPostProcessor.

questions.question: Should you cascade ALL on every association?
options: No—cascade only operations you intend to propagate; misuse causes accidental deletes/updates
questions.explanation: CascadeType.ALL propagates persist, merge, remove, refresh, detach. Apply narrowly—especially avoid careless REMOVE on wide graphs.

questions.question: Difference between BeanFactory and ApplicationContext?
options: ApplicationContext adds events, i18n, AOP autoproxy, and is what Spring Boot uses
questions.explanation: BeanFactory is minimal IoC. ApplicationContext is the rich container used in practice (AnnotationConfigApplicationContext, etc.).

questions.question: What is inversion of control (IoC)?
options: Framework controls object creation and wiring; app code receives dependencies
questions.explanation: IoC means the container manages lifecycle and dependencies rather than each class doing new Collaborator() itself.

questions.question: Why cache immutable reference data (countries, codes)?
options: Reduces database load for read-heavy stable data
questions.explanation: L2 cache shines for rarely changed lookup tables accessed across many requests—always tune eviction and consistency.

questions.question: What does DisposableBean interface provide?
options: destroy() callback for cleanup; prefer @PreDestroy in modern code
questions.explanation: Implementing DisposableBean defines destroy(). @PreDestroy is the annotation-based equivalent preferred in application code.

questions.question: How many PaymentService beans exist at runtime (default scope)?
options: One per injection point always new
questions.explanation: @Service defaults to singleton: one instance in the context; all injection points share it unless @Scope prototype.

questions.question: What is select fetch vs join fetch tradeoff?
options: Join fetch: one query, risk of duplicate rows; select: extra queries but simpler result set
questions.explanation: JOIN FETCH can duplicate parent rows with multiple children (use DISTINCT). SELECT fetch uses separate queries—batching mitigates N+1.

questions.question: What does @Primary resolve?
options: Default bean when multiple implementations match injection by type
questions.explanation: @Primary on one @Service implementation makes it the default when autowiring PaymentGateway without @Qualifier.

questions.question: What does @PropertySource add?
options: Loads properties file into Spring Environment
questions.explanation: @PropertySource("classpath:app.properties") exposes keys for @Value and Environment.getProperty().

questions.question: CascadeType.PERSIST vs REMOVE— which is safer to use widely?
options: PERSIST on parent-child composition where child has no meaning alone
Both on @ManyToOne from child to parent always
questions.explanation: PERSIST on composition (Order-LineItem) is common. REMOVE cascade from child to parent @ManyToOne is usually wrong and dangerous.
